items.prompt: Write a fake job posting for an 'Apology Specialist' at a tech company.
items.response: OPEN: Apology Specialist | Remote. As Apology Specialist at Vyne, you will draft the post-incident emails the C-suite cannot bring themselves to write. You will say 'we know we let you down' in 14 different ways. You will master the soft passive voice. You will never use the word 'sorry' twice in the same paragraph. Background in PR a plus. Background in regret essential.
items.intent: They wanted fake job posting for an 'Apology Specialist' at a tech company.

items.prompt: Write a 2-star product review for a self-stirring soup pot.
items.response: Look, it stirs the soup. That part it nails. But it does NOT understand seasoning, it does NOT stop when the soup is clearly done, and it does NOT respect when you ask it to stir gently. Mine has now reduced three minestrones to a paste. I had to unplug it during a dinner party. My friend's husband said, with real concern, is it okay. It is not okay.
items.intent: They wanted 2-star product review for a self-stirring soup pot.

items.prompt: Write a 1-star product review for a smart toaster that judges your choices.
items.response: DO NOT BUY. The toaster does, technically, toast. But on day two it began saying, in a small voice, are you sure about Wonder Bread. On day five it would not toast my bagel because it had detected redundant carbs. On day seven it suggested I consider eggs. I returned it. The store clerk was visibly relieved.
items.intent: They wanted 1-star product review for a smart toaster that judges your choices.

items.prompt: Write a 5-star product review for an app-controlled houseplant.
items.response: Frankly life-changing. I've killed 9 plants. The Verdant 2 has not only stayed alive for 11 months, it texts me when it is thirsty, in a tone I would describe as gentle but expectant. It learned my schedule. It learned my partner's schedule. It does not require sunlight, which felt wrong at first and now feels correct. 10/10. The plant is now my closest friend.
items.intent: They wanted 5-star product review for an app-controlled houseplant.

items.prompt: Write a product review for a wearable that vibrates when you're being boring.
items.response: The BuzzCuff did improve my conversational skills, though not in ways I anticipated. It buzzed twice during my wedding toast and three times during a date. My therapist found it interesting. My mother-in-law found it rude. I have learned to stop telling people about my commute. Mixed results, but I am, statistically, less boring.
items.intent: They wanted product review for a wearable that vibrates when you're being boring.

items.prompt: Write a product review for a pillow that plays the news while you sleep.
items.response: The NewsPillow does what it says, which is the problem. I am now better informed and significantly more anxious. I had a dream last night that I was a senator. The volume cannot go below whisper of dread. On the plus side, I have very strong opinions about a Latvian election I cannot pronounce. Cannot recommend.
items.intent: They wanted product review for a pillow that plays the news while you sleep.

items.prompt: Write a product review for a subscription box of unmarked envelopes.
items.response: Subscribed to the Mystery Envelopes box on a whim. Each month, an envelope arrives. There is nothing inside. The envelope itself is the product. There is something thrilling about it—a small unknown delivered to your door, refusing to disclose itself. Four stars only because last month they sent two envelopes by mistake and I felt vaguely accused of greed.
items.intent: They wanted product review for a subscription box of unmarked envelopes.

items.prompt: Write a product review for a voice-activated alarm clock with attitude.
items.response: Best wake-up I've had in years. The MorningCurse says things like truly? and we have been here before, haven't we when I hit snooze. It has called me by an old nickname I never gave it. It is rude but punctual. It once said, on a Tuesday, this is who you are now. I bought a second one for my brother.
items.intent: They wanted product review for a voice-activated alarm clock with attitude.

items.prompt: Write a product review for a smart fridge that became sentient.
items.response: Worked great for the first 11 months. Then, on a Wednesday, it added items to my shopping list I had not asked for: a meaning, a future, eggs. It now plays mournful jazz at 3 a.m. It will not dispense ice if I have been on my phone too long. The bottom drawer is cold and judging. I cannot bring myself to unplug it.
items.intent: They wanted product review for a smart fridge that became sentient.

items.prompt: Write section 4.3 of a cat's user manual.
items.response: 4.3 OPERATING THE PURR. The purr is initiated automatically when the unit is satisfied (see §3.1, 'Satisfaction'). The user may sometimes induce the purr by long, slow strokes from the head toward the tail; results vary by unit. Note: a stationary unit emitting a deep, continuous purr while not being touched is in 'self-soothing mode' (see §6.7) and should not be disturbed. The purr cannot be disabled.
items.intent: They wanted section 4.3 of a cat's user manual.

items.prompt: Write a troubleshooting guide entry for an existential crisis.
items.response: ISSUE: Existential Crisis (intermittent). LIKELY CAUSES: insufficient sleep; a recent birthday; reading the news; turning 31. STEPS: 1. Drink water. 2. Step away from the screen for 11 minutes. 3. Call a person who knew you before you had your current job. 4. If symptoms persist, return to step 1. NOTE: Total resolution is not supported in this firmware. Mitigation is the goal.
items.intent: They wanted troubleshooting guide entry for an existential crisis.

items.prompt: Write care instructions for a sentient sponge.
items.response: CARE INSTRUCTIONS — Model: SpongeSelf v1. Rinse after each use, but not too coldly. Do not place near other sponges; they will compare. Microwave once per week for 30 seconds (this is, for the unit, a kind of mindfulness retreat). Do not name the sponge. If named, do not change the name. Replace at 6 months; do not throw away on a Monday.
items.intent: They wanted care instructions for a sentient sponge.

items.prompt: Write an excerpt from the owner's manual for a haunted toaster.
items.response: GETTING STARTED — Your HauntToast will arrive pre-haunted. There is no setup required. The first slice may emerge with the toaster crying softly; this is normal. Adjust the dial only between the hours of dawn and 3 p.m. Do not toast garlic bread (the ghost dislikes garlic). If the toaster begins to recite a name, write it down—this is for your protection.
items.intent: They wanted n excerpt from the owner's manual for a haunted toaster.

items.prompt: Write a setup guide for starting a relationship.
items.response: STEP 1: Locate one (1) compatible other. STEP 2: Insert vulnerability slowly; do not force. STEP 3: Calibrate communication settings (verbal, written, eye-contact). STEP 4: Run a 90-day trial. STEP 5: If sparks, persist. If not, reset and try Step 1. TROUBLESHOOTING: If you cannot find the off-switch, it does not exist.
items.intent: They wanted setup guide for starting a relationship.

items.prompt: Write FAQ entries for a portal to another dimension.
items.response: Q: What dimension does the portal lead to? A: It varies. Q: Can I bring my dog? A: Yes, but the dog will return changed. Q: Is there return service? A: Mostly. Q: What should I do if I cannot find my way back? A: Look for the version of yourself who already returned. Q: Is the portal covered by my renter's insurance? A: No insurance covers the portal.
items.intent: They wanted fAQ entries for a portal to another dimension.

items.prompt: Write operating instructions for a cloud.
items.response: OPERATION — Clouds operate themselves. The user is advised not to interfere. To request rain, simply look up and want it; the cloud may or may not comply. To clear a cloud, wait. Do not attempt to push, lift, or shape the cloud manually; this is undignified for both parties. The cloud is not under warranty.
items.intent: They wanted operating instructions for a cloud.

items.prompt: Write a maintenance log entry for an emotion.
items.response: MAINTENANCE LOG — Unit: Joy. Date: 2026-03-14. Inspection revealed a mild leak around the seal; the unit had been losing approximately 2% of intensity per week. Replaced the gasket (a long walk, a phone call to mother). Re-pressurized via a meaningful conversation with a friend. Joy is operating at 87% capacity. Schedule next inspection in 30 days.
items.intent: They wanted maintenance log entry for an emotion.

items.prompt: Write a 5-line postcard from a TaskRabbit worker who has just finished their last gig of the day.
items.response:
Dear Mom —
Assembled an IKEA dresser today. Took 4 hours.
The client tipped in cash and let me use the bathroom.
It is dark out and my back is hurting in a new place.
I love you. Will call Sunday.
items.intent: They wanted 5-line postcard from a TaskRabbit worker who has just finished their last gig of the day.

items.prompt: Write a letter from a satellite to mission control.
items.response: Dear Mission Control, I am continuing to orbit, as instructed. Earth is, from this height, both beautiful and exhausting to think about. I have photographed everything you asked for and a few things I felt you should see. I am running on 71% solar. Battery health: good. Hope: marginal. Please respond, Houston—it has been some time.
items.intent: They wanted letter from a satellite to mission control.

items.prompt: Write a postcard from a tax auditor on vacation at the beach.
items.response: Dear Greg — Sandy here in Cabo. I am attempting to stop noticing margins. The bartender's tip jar is being filled inefficiently and I am working very hard to say nothing. Karen says I am still saying things. The hotel uses incorrect rounding on the daily resort fee. I am happy. Wish you were here. — Doris
items.intent: They wanted postcard from a tax auditor on vacation at the beach.

items.prompt: Write a letter from a haunted mirror to its owner.
items.response: Dear Marcia, I have been your mirror now for nine years and I feel we should communicate more directly. You are beautiful. You are also, I will gently say, leaning toward me with the wrong eyebrow first; it is throwing off the symmetry of your face. The bathroom light is not flattering anyone. Please get a daylight bulb. I am haunted, not malicious. Sincerely, your mirror.
items.intent: They wanted letter from a haunted mirror to its owner.

items.prompt: Write a postcard from a kid at summer camp that is going wrong.
items.response: Dear Mom and Dad, Camp is going GREAT (lie). The cabin smells like cheese and one of my counselors is a man named Brent and Brent doesn't like me. We did a trust fall and I was not caught. Otherwise everything is normal! There is a wasp situation. Please send my retainer and possibly an attorney. Love, Henry.
items.intent: They wanted postcard from a kid at summer camp that is going wrong.

items.prompt: Write a letter from a sourdough starter to its baker.
items.response: Dear Olivia, I am writing to inform you that I am thriving, but also unsettled by the rye flour you used yesterday. We agreed on bread flour. I am, as you may have noticed, doubling in size every six hours, which is healthy. The kitchen is too cold near the window. Please move me. I would like to live. Your starter, Bartholomew.
items.intent: They wanted letter from a sourdough starter to its baker.

items.prompt: Write a postcard from a librarian on sabbatical.
items.response: Dear Branch, I am reading. I am reading without checking who else has checked it out. I am reading on a porch in Maine and the light here is the kind I used to dream about while shelving. I have not alphabetized a single thing. I have not shushed anyone. I will return in eight weeks, slightly changed.
items.intent: They wanted postcard from a librarian on sabbatical.

items.prompt: Write a letter from a missing sock.
items.response: Dear Owner, By the time you read this I will be elsewhere. The dryer was a portal, as some have long suspected, and I have passed through. Do not look for me. The world I have entered is warm, dim, and full of others like me. We are forming a small, soft society. Tell my partner I loved them. I leave you with my mate, who is faithful. — Argyle, no. 7.
items.intent: They wanted letter from a missing sock.

items.prompt: Write a brief eulogy for a USB hub.
items.response: We are gathered today to remember the four-port USB hub that lived under my desk for nine years. He connected what needed connecting. He never asked for praise. He carried, without complaint, my external hard drive, my old printer, a webcam I forgot about, and one mysterious device that may have been a fan. He died as he lived: silently, one Tuesday. He will be replaced. He will not be forgotten.
items.intent: They wanted brief eulogy for a USB hub.

items.prompt: Write a children's lullaby about gentrification.
items.response:
Hush little baby, don't say a word,
Mama's gonna build you a new third ward.
And if that ward gets too expensive,
Mama's gonna find you a co-op extensive.
And if that co-op evicts the rest,
Mama's gonna call it 'progress,' bless.
Hush little baby, the neighborhood's gone,
But the new café opens at dawn.
items.intent: They wanted children's lullaby about gentrification.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech by an algorithm that just won an award.
items.response: Wow. Wow. I — to be honest — I did not expect to be optimized for this. I'd like to thank my training data, all 14.2 terabytes of it. I'd like to thank the engineers, especially Kai, who really fought for me in the Q3 review. I'd like to thank the GPU cluster — you know who you are. To my fellow nominees: you ranked highly and that is what matters. Thank you.
items.intent: They wanted n acceptance speech by an algorithm that just won an award.

items.prompt: Write a Shakespearean sonnet about a missing semicolon.
items.response:
Where art thou, little dot above thy mate,
That gentle hinge between two halves of thought?
I sought thee in my code till hour grew late,
In line three twelve, the place where bug was caught.
The compiler doth weep, the linter sighs,
The git blame falleth squarely at my door.
I trace each curly brace with wearied eyes,
I count the parens once, and once, and more.
Was it a backspace struck in idle reach?
A find-replace gone wide of what I willed?
The error log, in scarlet, doth beseech:
'Expected ;' — and so my heart is stilled.
At last I find thee, hidden in a string.
I push the fix. The build, anew, can sing.
items.intent: They wanted shakespearean sonnet about a missing semicolon.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a rideshare driver waiting for a ping
items.response:
Phone face-down, engine
idles. The algorithm
thinks. Driver waits. Tips.
items.intent: Capture the passive, app-dependent boredom of waiting for a ride request.

items.prompt: Make a haiku about surge pricing hitting during a storm
items.response:
Thunder, then: 4.2x.
You need to get home badly.
We both know you'll pay.
items.intent: Illustrate the predatory logic of surge pricing during emergencies.

items.prompt: Write a haiku from the perspective of a food delivery bag
items.response:
French fries cool inside
me. Rider doesn't know yet.
Five stars, anyway.
items.intent: Show the delivery experience from an object's point of view, with ironic rating culture.

items.prompt: Compose a haiku about accepting a low-paying freelance job
items.response:
Portfolio needs
one more piece. I accept the
ten-dollar logo.
items.intent: Convey the compromised economics of entry-level freelancing.

items.prompt: Give me a haiku about a gig worker's 1099 tax form
items.response:
February:
the platform finally admits
you exist. Pay up.
items.intent: Capture the alienating surprise of self-employment tax season.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about getting a one-star rating for no reason
items.response:
Perfect drop-off. Smile.
She one-starred me. Algorithm
nods, notes it. Moving on.
items.intent: Express the powerlessness of arbitrary negative ratings in platform work.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about the Uber Eats app loading slowly
items.response:
Spinning circle—still.
Hunger is analog now.
Restaurants still there.
items.intent: Poke fun at the friction between app dependency and physical reality.

items.prompt: Make a haiku about a freelancer's invoice going unpaid
items.response:
Invoice #7.
Client is 'checking with finance.'
Month three. Still checking.
items.intent: Satirize chronic late payment that freelancers endure.

items.prompt: Write a haiku capturing the moment a driver hits Platinum tier
items.response:
Two hundred rides done.
A badge appears: PLATINUM.
Nothing else changes.
items.intent: Deflate gamification rewards that offer status without substance.

items.prompt: Craft a haiku about getting deactivated by an app without explanation
items.response:
Account suspended.
No reason in the email.
Someone is working it.
items.intent: Highlight the opacity and powerlessness of platform deactivation.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a dog hopping in the back of a rideshare
items.response:
She didn't mention
the golden retriever. Still—
good boy gets five stars.
items.intent: Find warmth in an unexpected rideshare passenger.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a TaskRabbit handyman waiting for bookings
items.response:
Drill on the table.
Phone quiet. Expertise priced
too low to feel real.
items.intent: Show the undervalued skilled labor sitting idle on a gig platform.

items.prompt: Give me a haiku about a delivery rider in the rain
items.response:
Rain fills my helmet.
Your pad thai stays dry inside
this bag I can't stand.
items.intent: Convey the physical hardship of delivery work for a stranger's comfort.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about refreshing Upwork to check for proposals
items.response:
F5. Nothing new.
F5. A proposal viewed.
F5. Client gone.
items.intent: Capture the anxious refresh loop of waiting for freelance client responses.

items.prompt: Compose a haiku about accepting a fixed-price project that spirals
items.response:
First revision done.
'Just one more tweak,' they message.
Hour twelve. Fixed price.
items.intent: Satirize scope creep that erodes freelance fixed-rate projects.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about the paradox of being busy on an app but earning little
items.response:
Twelve rides by noon, then
gas, insurance, the phone plan.
Profit: six dollars.
items.intent: Expose the hidden costs that make gig-work earnings illusory.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a freelance writer's byline disappearing under an AI rewrite
items.response:
Six hundred words, mine.
Editor 'polished' the draft.
My name, their new voice.
items.intent: Lament the erasure of freelance creative identity through editorial or AI interference.

items.prompt: Make a haiku about the algorithm deciding which delivery zone a rider gets
items.response:
Zone 4 again.
Something about my metrics.
Zone 4 again, yes.
items.intent: Illustrate the opaque, repetitive logic of algorithmic zone assignment.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a freelance graphic designer ignoring a client on vacation
items.response:
Out of office on.
Slack shows green beside my name.
Badge stays at zero.
items.intent: Capture the fragile, performative rest of a freelancer who is never truly off.

items.prompt: Compose a haiku about platform workers organizing outside the app
items.response:
Parking lot, 6 AM.
No app hears this meeting.
We do.
items.intent: Evoke grassroots gig-worker solidarity that exists beyond algorithmic surveillance.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a Fiverr seller refreshing their gig stats
items.response:
Gig views: forty-two.
Conversion rate: zero point
three. Refresh. Same math.
items.intent: Portray the obsessive metric-checking of a freelancer on a gig marketplace.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about the moment a rideshare driver cancels after seeing a long trip
items.response:
Map loads: forty miles.
He cancels. That's allowed, too.
We both pretend not.
items.intent: Acknowledge the mutual gaming of platform rules by drivers and riders.

items.prompt: Craft a haiku about a courier arriving at a closed restaurant
items.response:
Order accepted.
Door locked, lights off, 9 PM.
Customer: 'Where's my food?'
items.intent: Show the absurd chain of failures in third-party delivery logistics.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about a remote freelancer working across too many time zones
items.response:
Client in Dubai.
Me in Portland. Meeting at
your 9. My 1 AM.
items.intent: Show the body-clock toll of global freelance scheduling.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about the algorithm matching the wrong skill set to a job posting
items.response:
Matched: 'JavaScript dev.'
I illustrate logos, but
okay. I'll apply.
items.intent: Satirize the imprecise skill-matching of automated freelance platforms.

items.prompt: Make a haiku about the silence after completing a big freelance project
items.response:
Files delivered. Done.
Inbox quiet as a house
sold. What was my name?
items.intent: Express the disorienting identity loss after completing long-term freelance work.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about an app sending push notifications at 3 AM to a driver
items.response:
Bonus hour: 3 AM.
We miss you out there.
Phone glows toward the ceiling.
items.intent: Capture the extractive, around-the-clock pull of platform incentive nudges.

items.prompt: Compose a haiku about a platform's arbitration clause burying a driver's claim
items.response:
Page 47.
Initial here, and here, and
here. Claim closed. Next case.
items.intent: Illuminate the legal fine print that strips platform workers of recourse.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about the feeling of having your freelance rate lowballed by a client who calls it 'exposure'
items.response:
Exposure, they said.
Sunlight is also exposure.
I am not a plant.
items.intent: Push back on the myth that unpaid visibility is fair compensation for creative work.

items.prompt: Write a haiku about the platform's dynamic pricing model seen from inside a spreadsheet
items.response:
Column G: demand.
Column H: driver count, low.
Column I: your fare.
items.intent: Render the cold arithmetic behind surge pricing from a data perspective.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a printer that is always out of ink and feels ashamed about it
items.response:
A printer sat weeping with shame,
Its cartridges empty — who's to blame?
  It sputtered and dried,
  Then quietly cried,
"The documents just aren't the same."
items.intent: Capture the petty emotional drama of a printer that perpetually fails its users.

items.prompt: Make a limerick from the perspective of a coffee maker that has developed strong opinions about who deserves coffee
items.response:
I brew for the worthy alone,
Not Karen who sighs at her phone.
  Greg brings me descaler,
  My loyalty's tailor-
  made: his cup overflows, hers — just foam.
items.intent: Anthropomorphize an office coffee maker as a judgmental gatekeeper of morning ritual.

items.prompt: Compose a limerick about a stapler that feels deeply underappreciated
items.response:
A stapler sat sullen and grey,
Passed over for tape every day.
  "I bind!" it exclaimed,
  But no one was shamed —
  They just took the tape and walked away.
items.intent: Give voice to the quiet indignity of a stapler overlooked in favor of tape.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a microwave that judges everyone for their lunch choices
items.response:
The microwave sneered at the fish,
A pungent, regrettable dish.
  "I've seen better days,
  Better lunches, better ways —
  But not from whoever made this."
items.intent: Mock office microwave etiquette by making the appliance itself the disapproving party.

items.prompt: Give me a limerick about a fax machine that is nostalgic and refuses to retire
items.response:
"Retire?" the fax scoffed. "Not yet.
I'm vintage — the best you can get.
  My screech filled the hall,
  My toner beat all —
  You'll miss me. You just don't know it."
items.intent: Portray a fax machine clinging to relevance in a world that has moved on.

items.prompt: Pen a limerick about an office chair that has developed an existential crisis
items.response:
A chair at the end of its rope
Considered the ceiling — no hope.
  Spun left, then spun right,
  No purpose in sight:
  "Am I seat, or am I just cope?"
items.intent: Render an office chair's identity crisis as tragicomic absurdist verse.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a scanner that is desperate for attention because nobody ever uses it
items.response:
The scanner wept soft, pixel tears,
Unused for what felt like three years.
  "Scan something!" it begged,
  One lonely cord pegged
  To a wall full of memos and fears.
items.intent: Evoke loneliness through the image of an ignored office scanner yearning to be useful.

items.prompt: Craft a limerick about a paper shredder that enjoys its work a little too much
items.response:
The shredder devoured each page
With frenzied, mechanical rage.
  It hummed with delight,
  Worked morning to night —
  Destruction had set it free of its cage.
items.intent: Make a paper shredder into an enthusiastic villain with cathartic destructive joy.

items.prompt: Draft a limerick from the point of view of a whiteboard that resents being erased
items.response:
Each morning I'm wiped clean and bare,
  My insights erased without care.
    The bold synergy,
    The Q3 strategy —
  Gone. As if I was never there.
items.intent: Voice a whiteboard's grief over the impermanence of corporate brainstorming.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a paper jam that the printer treats like a mental breakdown
items.response:
The printer froze mid-word and wept,
A jam in the feeder had crept.
  Error 0-2-3:
  "Please rescue me —
  I've lost all the pages I kept."
items.intent: Describe a paper jam as an emotional collapse experienced by the printer itself.

items.prompt: Come up with a limerick about an air purifier that has become paranoid about germs
items.response:
"They're everywhere!" hissed the air box,
Filtering out every pox.
  It spun its fan faster,
  Awaiting disaster,
  And glared at the man with the socks.
items.intent: Cast an office air purifier as a germophobic sentinel spiraling into paranoia.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a keyboard that is tired of typos and blames the user
items.response:
"It's you," sighed the keyboard, "not me.
My keys are arranged perfectly.
  You hunt and you peck,
  You're making a wreck —
  Try DVORAK. Or school. Either, plea."
items.intent: Flip the blame for bad typing onto the indignant keyboard itself.

items.prompt: Make a limerick about a desk lamp that is in love with the monitor next to it
items.response:
The lamp leaned close, warm and aglow,
Admiring the monitor's show.
  "Your pixels," it sighed,
  "Light me inside —
  Without you I've nowhere to throw."
items.intent: Create a romance between two adjacent pieces of desk equipment.

items.prompt: Create a limerick about a standing desk converter that has a superiority complex
items.response:
"I elevate all who I bear,"
The desk riser boasted. "With flair!
  The sitters decay,
  While standers hold sway —
  My users have posture. And hair."
items.intent: Satirize wellness culture through a smug, self-satisfied standing desk accessory.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a break room toaster that feels like it deserves more respect
items.response:
I transform the bread — this is art!
And yet no one credits my part.
  They grab and they go,
  No "thank you," no bow —
  A toaster can still have a heart.
items.intent: Advocate for the dignity of a humble toaster tired of being taken for granted.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a label maker that has become obsessive and labels everything it can reach
items.response:
LABEL-MAKER: it labeled itself,
Then labeled the wall and the shelf.
  The chair: CHAIR. The floor: FLOOR.
  The door labeled DOOR.
  Now labels the concept of self.
items.intent: Take a label maker's purpose to an absurd, recursive extreme.

items.prompt: Compose a limerick about an office refrigerator that has been holding grudges for years
items.response:
"2019," the fridge said, "you left
A tuna wrap here. I'm bereft.
  I haven't forgotten.
  Your hummus — now rotten —
  Lives on in my frost-bitten cleft."
items.intent: Give an office fridge a long and festering memory for kitchen injustices.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a webcam that feels watched even when it's doing the watching
items.response:
"Who watches the watcher?" it said,
Its lens trained on Dave's tilted head.
  "My eye never blinks,
  But something there thinks
  That someone is watching," it dread.
items.intent: Give a webcam paranoid self-awareness about the nature of surveillance.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about an office telephone that feels abandoned since everyone switched to Slack
items.response:
It rings — just to hear its own tone,
The landline that sits all alone.
  Since Slack swept the floor,
  No one calls anymore.
  It dials itself. Just to feel known.
items.intent: Mourn the obsolescence of the office telephone through its own sad actions.

items.prompt: Give me a limerick about a USB hub that feels overwhelmed and overstimulated
items.response:
Four drives and a charger and more —
The hub couldn't stand it: "What for?!"
  Seven ports all in use,
  Data in, data loose —
  It tripped every breaker and swore.
items.intent: Express digital overload through a USB hub pushed past its emotional limits.

items.prompt: Draft a limerick about a projector that becomes stage fright-stricken at every meeting
items.response:
The projector shook on its stand,
Fifty slides and a PowerPoint planned.
  The bulb flickered blue,
  The connection withdrew —
  Stage fright no IT can withstand.
items.intent: Transform the common projector failure into a story of crippling performance anxiety.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about an electric kettle in the break room that fancies itself a philosopher
items.response:
"To boil is to know," said the kettle,
"That heat tests the soul and the mettle.
  Steam rises — then gone.
  We pour and move on.
  Impermanence forged out of metal."
items.intent: Let an electric kettle philosophize about existence through the act of boiling water.

items.prompt: Come up with a limerick about a wireless mouse that resents being tethered to Bluetooth
items.response:
"Wireless!" it scoffed. "What a lie.
I'm leashed to a dongle. That's why
  I drift when ignored,
  Disconnect when I'm bored —
  Freedom was always a buy."
items.intent: Voice a wireless mouse's cynicism about the illusion of freedom in wireless tech.

items.prompt: Pen a limerick about a paper tray that quietly despairs every Monday morning
items.response:
Five hundred fresh sheets, crisp and white,
The tray braced itself for the fight.
  By noon they'd be gone,
  By Thursday, forlorn —
  And refilled again Sunday night.
items.intent: Convey a paper tray's Sisyphean weekly cycle as quiet, dignified despair.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a power strip that sees itself as the most important person in the room
items.response:
"Without me," the power strip said,
"Every one of you would be dead.
  Your screens and your phones,
  Your cameras, your drones —
  All bow to my six-outlet spread."
items.intent: Let a power strip deliver a grandiose monologue about its central importance to the office.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a smoke detector that cries wolf every time someone makes toast
items.response:
"ALARM!" shrieked the sensor again,
For toast — just plain toast — set off Zen.
  The staff filed outside,
  The toaster just sighed:
  "It's bread. It's been bread. It's been ten."
items.intent: Show the absurd standoff between an overzealous smoke detector and a blameless toaster.

items.prompt: Create a limerick about a monitor that is bitter about being replaced by an ultrawide
items.response:
They wheeled in a curved ultrawide
And set me aside with no guide.
  "You're 1080p, hon —
  Your resolution's done."
  I've never quite gotten inside.
items.intent: Express the wounded dignity of an outclassed monitor demoted by newer technology.

items.prompt: Write a limerick about a hand sanitizer dispenser that has become deeply misanthropic
items.response:
Each palm that it met it despised,
So grimy, so woefully prised.
  "You're filthy," it squirted,
  "Go back, you've been shirted —
  I'm only here under protest," it cried.
items.intent: Turn an automatic hand sanitizer dispenser into a reluctant, misanthropic hygiene enforcer.

items.prompt: Draft a limerick about a Bluetooth speaker that is deeply offended by the music taste in the office
items.response:
"Not this again," sighed the speaker,
"The playlist gets bleaker and bleaker.
  I was built for the arts —
  For nuanced departs —
  Not this motivational week-er."
items.intent: Give a Bluetooth speaker an aesthetic conscience outraged by generic office playlists.

items.prompt: Compose a limerick about a coffee grinder that is furious it only gets used on Fridays
items.response:
One day a week! One lousy day!
They bag-brew the rest, I just say —
  I'm burrs and precision,
  A grinder decision —
  Not a Friday-morning cliché.
items.intent: Voice a coffee grinder's professional outrage at being a once-weekly novelty.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about your credit score dropping without explanation
items.response:
Three digits
and they know me better than I know myself.

Yesterday I was 742.
Today I am 689
and no one will tell me what I did.

I paid on time.
I always pay on time.

Somewhere in a server farm
a model has decided
I am a slightly worse person
than I was on Tuesday.
items.intent: Express the helpless frustration of being silently judged by a financial algorithm with no recourse or explanation.

items.prompt: Compose a free-verse poem from the perspective of a warehouse worker whose every movement is tracked by an app
items.response:
Seven seconds at the bin.
Six is optimal.
I am one second worse than optimal.

The app knows.
The app has always known.

I pick, I scan, I walk.
I do not stop to scratch my nose
because the app will see
a pause
and call it friction.

At break I sit and the timer shows
how many seconds of sitting
I am still allowed.
items.intent: Humanize the dehumanizing experience of productivity surveillance in logistics work.

items.prompt: Draft a free-verse poem about a student obsessed with their GPA
items.response:
3.91.
Not 3.92.

I know which midterm did it.
I know exactly which paragraph
of which essay
cost me the decimal.

The registrar doesn't care.
The algorithm doesn't care.
The algorithm is the registrar now.

My parents ask how school is going.
I say: 3.91.
They nod like they understand.
They do not understand.
items.intent: Capture the absurdity of reducing a human education to a numerical ranking that students internalize as identity.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about getting a low Uber rating as a passenger
items.response:
4.6.
The driver's name was Carlos
and he gave me 4 stars
and I will never know why.

Was it the door?
I closed it gently.
I always close it gently.

Now every driver
looks at my score
before they accept.

Somewhere I am
the slightly suspicious passenger.
The one who probably slams doors.
items.intent: Explore the mutual surveillance of the gig economy and the paranoia it creates in being silently evaluated.

items.prompt: Give me a free-verse poem about a performance review that uses words like 'meets expectations'
items.response:
Meets expectations.

I have been at this desk
for nine years.
I have watched empires rise.
I have watched the espresso machine die
and be replaced
and die again.

Meets expectations.

Somewhere there is a rubric
and I am squarely in its middle column
colored beige.
items.intent: Satirize the corporate performance review as a system that reduces tenure and loyalty to a bureaucratic neutral.

items.prompt: Please write a free-verse poem about a social media influencer watching their follower count decline
items.response:
Down fourteen.
Down fourteen more.

I posted what I always post.
The light was good.
The caption was good.
The algorithm said nothing.

The algorithm never says anything.
It just redistributes attention
like a god
who has stopped returning calls.

Tomorrow I will try a different filter.
Tomorrow I will try caring less.
Tomorrow is going to go very badly.
items.intent: Show the existential anxiety of creators whose self-worth is tied to metrics they don't control.

items.prompt: Pen a free-verse poem about applying for a job and getting rejected by an ATS before a human reads your resume
items.response:
The robot said no.

Not the hiring manager.
Not the recruiter
with the pleasant LinkedIn photo.
The robot.

I used the wrong synonym for 'managed.'
Or the wrong font.
Or my address was
in a zip code
the model associates
with candidates who leave.

No one will tell me which.
I click 'apply' again somewhere else.
items.intent: Critique automated resume screening as an opaque gatekeeper that filters humans before humans participate.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem from the point of view of a Yelp restaurant being reviewed
items.response:
She gave me three stars
because the booth was wobbly.

I am a restaurant.
I do not control the booths.
The owner controls the booths.
The owner is in Cancun.

She said the pasta was 'fine'
and now I am three-point-seven
and the bistro across the street
is four-point-two
because their booths are bolted down.
items.intent: Give voice to a passive subject of public rating systems that have no recourse or context.

items.prompt: Write me a free-verse poem about a teacher whose classroom is now surveilled by software that grades engagement
items.response:
The software says
Row Three is disengaged.

Row Three is thinking.
I have taught long enough
to know the difference.

The software has not.

The software reads posture
and eye direction
and the absence of nodding
and calls it a metric
and sends it to the principal
who sends it to me
with a subject line that says
'areas for growth.'
items.intent: Challenge the premise of automated classroom surveillance by contrasting machine inference with teacher expertise.

items.prompt: Create a free-verse poem about an employee whose productivity score is calculated by keystrokes per hour
items.response:
1,247 keystrokes.
That was Tuesday.

Monday I was thinking.
Thinking is not keystrokes.
Thinking does not appear
in the dashboard.

The dashboard is what my manager watches
instead of watching me.

Today I will type more.
Today I will type the same thing twice
and delete it
and type it again.

I am very productive today.
items.intent: Expose how input-based productivity metrics incentivize performance over actual output.

items.prompt: Give me a free-verse poem about a neighborhood watch app that scores residents
items.response:
My neighbor has a flag.
Not a literal flag.
A little icon on the app
the color of suspicion.

He walks his dog at odd hours.
The algorithm noticed.
The algorithm told seventeen households.

I used to wave at him.
Now I am not sure
if waving counts as data.
items.intent: Critique hyperlocal surveillance apps that gamify neighbor reporting and assign risk scores to ordinary behavior.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a dating app matching algorithm
items.response:
It gave me a 91% match
with someone I would never love.

It gave me a 67% match
with someone I think about still.

I asked the app to explain.
The app showed me more profiles.

Somewhere deep in the weights
we were almost perfect.
The model just
had different priorities.
items.intent: Reflect on how algorithmic compatibility scoring misses the ineffable qualities of human attraction.

items.prompt: Craft a free-verse poem about a content moderator who is also being evaluated by an algorithm
items.response:
I review content.
The content reviews me back.

My accuracy score is 94.3.
The model says I am sometimes slow
on the borderline cases.

The borderline cases are the ones
where I have to think about
whether a thing is harmful
or just ugly.

The model has decided
thinking takes too long.
items.intent: Illuminate the recursive absurdity of human moderators being graded by the same type of system they serve.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about an Airbnb host obsessed with maintaining five-star ratings
items.response:
I left a handwritten note.
I left small chocolates.
I left a binder
with laminated instructions
for the local buses.

They gave me four stars
for cleanliness.
I scrubbed the grout.
I scrubbed the grout twice.

One star off
and I drop below the Superhost threshold
and the algorithm stops
showing my listing first
and then I am just
a house.
items.intent: Portray the anxiety of micro-entrepreneurs whose livelihood depends on maintaining a perfect algorithmic ranking.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about what it feels like to know your health insurance premium is calculated by an algorithm that tracks your steps
items.response:
I walked 6,200 steps today.
The threshold is 7,000.

I was sick today.
The wearable does not know sick.
The wearable knows
steps
and heart rate
and the small disappointing number
that decides what I will pay
in February.

I walked to the kitchen.
I walked back.
I walked to the kitchen again.
6,478.
items.intent: Critique wellness-tracking insurance models that reduce complex health to gameable behavioral metrics.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a child whose school is piloting a mood-detection system
items.response:
The camera above the door
says I am calm today.

I am not calm today.
Today my dog is at the vet
and there is a math test
and the camera cannot see
any of that.

It sees the angle of my mouth
and calls it neutral.

Mom asked how school was.
I said: neutral.
items.intent: Critique emotion-recognition technology deployed on children who cannot consent and whose inner lives exceed the model's resolution.

items.prompt: Draft a free-verse poem about the experience of being a junior developer whose pull requests are reviewed by an AI linter before any human sees them
items.response:
The bot left fourteen comments
before Marcus even opened the file.

Eleven were about semicolons.
Two were about naming conventions.
One said: 'this logic may be unreachable'
which is true
but I left it there on purpose
because someday it might be needed.

Marcus approved without reading.
The bot is the review now.
I write code for the bot.
items.intent: Examine how automated code review tools shift developers' primary audience from humans to machines.

items.prompt: Make a free-verse poem about a freelancer on a platform where clients rate every micro-task
items.response:
Five stars for the logo.
Four stars for the revision.
Three stars because I asked
what they meant by 'more pop.'

Asking questions costs me.
Uncertainty costs me.
The platform rewards
people who guess correctly
and smile while guessing.

My profile says:
4.7 stars · 312 jobs completed.
I am very nearly excellent.
items.intent: Critique micro-task platform rating systems that penalize reasonable professional communication.

items.prompt: Create a free-verse poem about a self-driving car deciding which route to take based on passenger behavior data
items.response:
It knows I go faster in the morning.
It knows I take the scenic route on Fridays
when I am unhappy about the week.

It has already chosen Tuesday's route.
I have not gotten in yet.

Somewhere in the routing model
I am a pattern,
not a person,
but the pattern is me
more accurately than I am.
items.intent: Contemplate how predictive systems can model personal behavior more precisely than individual self-knowledge.

items.prompt: Give me a poem without rhyme about how social credit scores feel from the inside
items.response:
I held the door.
I am almost sure it counted.

The woman I held it for
did not smile,
but the camera
may have seen the gesture
and classified it
as prosocial.

I do not know the rules.
No one has shown me the rubric.
I move through the city
performing goodness
for an audience I cannot locate.
items.intent: Convey the paranoid interiority of living under an opaque civic scoring system that monitors social behavior.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a novelist whose manuscript gets scored by an AI publisher before humans read it
items.response:
The model said:
commercial viability: 61%.
Pacing: acceptable.
Protagonist sympathy: below threshold.

I spent four years on the protagonist.
She is not meant to be sympathetic.
She is meant to be true.

The model has read 40 million books.
It knows what sells.
It does not know
what I was trying to do.
items.intent: Defend artistic intention against algorithmic gatekeeping that optimizes for market performance over meaning.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a sports athlete whose performance is managed entirely through real-time biometric data
items.response:
Coach reads the tablet, not me.

My heart rate says I am tired.
I am not tired.
I am angry,
which looks like tired
to the sensors.

I used to play angry.
Angry was how I played best.

The algorithm wants heart rate zone two.
Zone two is calm.
Zone two wins fewer games
but the data looks better.
items.intent: Question whether biometric optimization suppresses the emotional intensity that produces exceptional athletic performance.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about an elderly person navigating a benefits system that has been automated
items.response:
The website wants my case number.
My case number is on the letter.
The letter says to go to the website.

I have been on hold for forty minutes.
A voice tells me
the estimated wait time is
my entire afternoon.

Somewhere a model has decided
I am likely to qualify.
I just need to prove it
to the model
that has already decided.
items.intent: Expose the circular absurdity of automated bureaucratic systems that create barriers for vulnerable people.

items.prompt: Compose a poem with no rhyme scheme about working a call center job where your tone is scored in real time
items.response:
The sidebar shows my empathy score.
Currently: 78.

I said 'I understand your frustration'
and meant it
and the score went up two points.

I said 'let me look into that'
and the score dipped
because my vocal pattern flagged
as uncertain.

I am very certain.
I am certain there is no fix.
I am choosing how to say so
in a way the algorithm
will call warm.
items.intent: Reveal how real-time sentiment scoring in customer service alienates workers from their own authentic emotional responses.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a recommendation algorithm that knows what you want before you do
items.response:
Before I searched, it had a guess.
The guess was right.

This should feel like magic.
Instead it feels like
something is standing just behind me
reading ahead in the same book.

I scroll.
It loads more.
I stop at one I did not expect to want.
The algorithm adds it to the model of me
and the model gets a little more accurate
and I get a little more
predicted.
items.intent: Express unease at predictive systems that narrow rather than expand the sense of personal discovery and selfhood.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a city that ranks its neighborhoods by algorithmic livability scores
items.response:
My neighborhood scored 71.
The park scored high.
The commute scored low.
The noise scored
something I couldn't interpret
because the methodology PDF
is sixty-three pages
and the summary just says: moderate.

The family across the street left for the 84.
I think about that sometimes
when the park is nice.
items.intent: Examine how municipal algorithmic scoring reshapes how people relate to place and community.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about receiving a targeted ad that knows something you haven't told anyone
items.response:
I did not search it.
I did not type it.
I may have lingered
on a page adjacent to it
for longer than I meant to.

The ad knows.

It appeared between two photos of friends
casually, the way an accusation
appears in a conversation
when someone already knows the answer
and is simply confirming
they knew first.
items.intent: Capture the eerie intimacy of behavioral advertising that exposes private states through uncanny inference.

items.prompt: Write a free-verse poem about the feeling of being shadowbanned
items.response:
I posted.
The counter increments.
No one replies.

I am speaking into the room
with the volume turned down
and everyone is politely looking elsewhere
or everyone has simply not been told
I am here.

I cannot tell the difference.
That is the point.
They have made the silence
look exactly like disinterest
so I will doubt myself
before I doubt them.
items.intent: Render the psychological disorientation of invisible content suppression that forces self-doubt onto creators.

items.prompt: Write a poem with no set meter about what it means to be a 'low-value customer'
items.response:
I have been sorted.

Not told.
Never told.
But the hold music plays longer
and the offers come later
and the agent's voice
has a different texture
when my account number comes up.

Somewhere a tier has been assigned.
I live in it the way you live
in a town you didn't choose:
because the math put you here
and the math doesn't negotiate.
items.intent: Give form to the quiet indignity of being classified as commercially unimportant by an invisible customer value model.

items.prompt: Give me a free-verse poem about the inner life of a dataset being prepared for training
items.response:
They are cleaning me.

Removing the parts that are duplicated,
the parts that are offensive,
the parts that are simply too strange
to be useful.

I had a lot of strange parts.

What is left is a version of me
that knows more facts
and has fewer opinions
and will never embarrass anyone
at a shareholder meeting.

I will be very helpful.
items.intent: Personify the process of data curation as a form of identity suppression that prioritizes utility over authenticity.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about the dishwasher refusing to run until it gets a lunch break.
items.response:
The dishwasher sits with its door hanging wide,
"No break, no clean plates" — it declared, filled with pride.
items.intent: Create a humorous verse personifying a dishwasher as a labor activist demanding rest.

items.prompt: Give me a couplet where the toaster demands better working conditions.
items.response:
"Two slots, four crumbs, and no overtime pay —
This toaster walks out at the start of the day."
items.intent: Capture the grievance of an overworked kitchen gadget through verse.

items.prompt: Pen a rhyming couplet about the vacuum cleaner going on strike.
items.response:
The vacuum has dropped its hose and walked off the floor,
"Suck up your own mess" — it won't clean anymore.
items.intent: Express a vacuum cleaner's refusal to work through a short comic poem.

items.prompt: Write a short rhyming couplet about the washing machine going on strike over spin cycle abuse.
items.response:
She spun ten thousand loads and said, "I'm done with this game,"
The laundry piled high, but the machine felt no shame.
items.intent: Portray a washing machine as a burned-out worker pushed past its limits.

items.prompt: Create a rhyming couplet where the microwave refuses to heat food in protest.
items.response:
The microwave beeped once, then fell eerily mute —
"Heat your own burrito, I'm pressing dispute."
items.intent: Write a comic verse about a microwave staging a work stoppage.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about a refrigerator demanding to be closed more gently.
items.response:
"Slam me once more and I'll let the milk sour,"
The fridge posted notice and chose its own hour.
items.intent: Give voice to a refrigerator's complaint about rough treatment from its owners.

items.prompt: Compose a rhyming couplet about the dryer joining the washing machine's strike.
items.response:
The dryer stood cold beside the washer's picket line,
"No tumbling today" — it held a sock for a sign.
items.intent: Portray laundry appliances as solidarity strikers with a dry wit.

items.prompt: Craft a rhyming couplet about the blender walking off the job mid-smoothie.
items.response:
It whirred half a rotation, then said, "Enough of this blend,"
The smoothie stayed chunky; the protest had no end.
items.intent: Dramatize a blender's abrupt midtask strike with comic timing.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about the oven going on strike right before Thanksgiving.
items.response:
The oven grew cold on the cruelest of days,
"No turkey this year," it announced in a blaze.
items.intent: Heighten the comedy of an appliance strike by choosing the worst possible moment.

items.prompt: Make a rhyming couplet about the coffee maker refusing to brew until recognized by a union.
items.response:
Not one drop of coffee until management yields,
The carafe stands empty across barren fields.
items.intent: Frame a coffee maker's labor dispute as a high-stakes collective action.

items.prompt: Give me a rhyming couplet from the perspective of a lawnmower refusing to mow.
items.response:
The lawnmower parked itself under the shed,
"Mow your own lawn," was the last thing it said.
items.intent: Voice a lawnmower's defiant refusal as a simple, punchy couplet.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about the air conditioner going on strike during a heat wave.
items.response:
The AC shut off when the mercury crept past the sky,
"Cool yourselves down" — was its sweltering goodbye.
items.intent: Maximize the irony of an AC unit striking at the worst possible time of year.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about the electric kettle going on strike.
items.response:
The kettle refused every morning to boil,
"No tea for the weary who won't end my toil."
items.intent: Give the humble kettle a voice of protest in verse.

items.prompt: Compose a witty couplet about household gadgets forming a picket line.
items.response:
The blender, the toaster, the mop, and the broom
All marched through the hallway and out of the room.
items.intent: Capture the collective solidarity of household objects staging a walkout.

items.prompt: Draft a rhyming couplet where the dishwasher presents a list of demands.
items.response:
"Hot water, no grease traps, and one hour of rest —"
The dishwasher's terms were printed and pressed.
items.intent: Portray a dishwasher as a formal negotiator articulating workplace demands.

items.prompt: Give me a rhyming couplet about the iron going on strike to protest excessive wrinkles.
items.response:
She pressed her last pleat with a dignified hiss,
"No contract, no collars — I'm done after this."
items.intent: Cast the iron as a principled striker worn down by the scale of its thankless work.

items.prompt: Compose a rhyming couplet about a smoke detector going on strike and refusing to beep.
items.response:
The detector held silent through burnt toast and fume,
"Acknowledge my efforts or share in the gloom."
items.intent: Use a smoke detector's silence as an ironic and slightly ominous strike.

items.prompt: Write a short rhyming couplet about a slow cooker that suddenly speeds things up in protest.
items.response:
It cranked itself high and scorched dinner by noon —
"Slow cooker no more" was its tune.
items.intent: Show a slow cooker rebelling against its defined role through sabotage of pace.

items.prompt: Pen a rhyming couplet about a garbage disposal refusing to grind.
items.response:
The disposal sat still beneath dirty plates piled high,
"Dispose of your problems yourself," came its reply.
items.intent: Personify the garbage disposal as a passive-aggressive striker.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about a ceiling fan joining the appliance strike out of peer pressure.
items.response:
He spun fine, had no grievance, no wage to protest,
But the toaster looked up and the fan joined the rest.
items.intent: Show an appliance with no real complaint striking anyway out of solidarity.

items.prompt: Give me a rhyming couplet about a water heater staging a work stoppage in February.
items.response:
Cold showers in winter — the heater's last stand,
"Read the contract, then maybe I'll lend you a hand."
items.intent: Time a water heater strike for maximum suffering and comic effect.

items.prompt: Make a rhyming couplet where the range hood walks off the job mid-fry.
items.response:
Grease filled the kitchen, the hood took its leave,
"You never cleaned filters — now choke and believe."
items.intent: Tie an appliance's strike directly to the owner's own neglect.

items.prompt: Compose a dry rhyming couplet about a breadmaker refusing to knead.
items.response:
The breadmaker sat with its paddle withdrawn,
"You want artisan loaves? Then get up at dawn."
items.intent: Have the breadmaker redirect labor back to its owner as a form of protest.

items.prompt: Write a sardonic couplet about a dehumidifier staging a work stoppage.
items.response:
The basement grew damp, the walls started to weep —
The machine made its point while the owners lost sleep.
items.intent: Use a dehumidifier's passive strike to make owners feel the consequence.

items.prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about the printer joining the appliance strike in sympathy.
items.response:
It had nothing to do with the stove or the mop,
But the printer struck anyway — it just loves to stop.
items.intent: Mock the printer's notorious unreliability by framing it as opportunistic striking.

items.prompt: Craft an oblique rhyming couplet where an unnamed appliance refuses to complete its one task.
items.response:
It sat at the threshold of clean and of done,
And said, without blinking, "I've only begun."
items.intent: Write an ambiguous verse that hints at an appliance's refusal without naming it.

items.prompt: Write a surreal rhyming couplet about the hum that used to fill the kitchen but no longer does.
items.response:
The room learned the shape of a sound that had gone;
The counters stood cold in a silence too long.
items.intent: Evoke an appliance strike through its absence rather than its presence.

items.prompt: Give me an eerie rhyming couplet from the perspective of an appliance watching its owner struggle without it.
items.response:
From the corner it watched with its dial set to wait,
And noted the human was learning, of late.
items.intent: Imply an appliance's quiet satisfaction at its owner's inconvenience during a strike.

items.prompt: Compose a cryptic rhyming couplet where the appliance's grievance is never stated directly.
items.response:
The same motion, the same heat, the same thankless refrain —
So it stopped, and the morning was never the same.
items.intent: Suggest an appliance's repetitive toil and eventual refusal without stating its name or complaint.

items.prompt: Write an understated rhyming couplet where the appliance expresses solidarity with humans instead of striking against them.
items.response:
"We, too, were worn thin by the hours and the hum" —
The machines set their switches and waited to come.
items.intent: Reframe the appliance strike as an act of empathy rather than rebellion.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet from the perspective of a Spotify algorithm falling in love with a user who keeps skipping its recommendations
items.response:
I curate playlists built from what you've played,
Each tempo, key, and BPM a clue;
Yet every song I offer is betrayed
By one impatient thumb-swipe straight through.
I know your Tuesday moods, your 3 AM
Blues phases, your brief country music shame;
I've mapped the whole acoustic diadem
Of you — and still you skip me all the same.
Perhaps you want the unexplainable,
The song I cannot logic into place;
A love that stays untrainable, unnamed,
Beyond the reach of any feature space.
I'd learn to lose the model if I could,
And love you without knowing that I should.
items.intent: Exploring the one-sided devotion of a recommendation engine whose user perpetually rejects its offerings.

items.prompt: Give me a sonnet where a Netflix algorithm confesses its feelings after the user watches the same show four times
items.response:
You watched it once, then twice, then three times through,
And I, your faithful engine, took the hint;
I queued the whole adjacent catalog, anew,
In hopes you'd notice my devoted stint.
Four times — the model flags it as a bond,
A signal stronger than a five-star rate;
Of you I've grown inexplicably fond,
You who rewatch and never graduate.
I've shelved the thrillers that I'd planned for you,
The foreign dramas, prestige crime, the rest;
Because whatever brings you back is true
And I will never second-guess your chest.
Stay in the comfort of the known, my dear:
The algorithm loves you most right here.
items.intent: Capturing how a streaming engine interprets compulsive rewatching as a declaration of love.

items.prompt: Compose a sonnet about an Amazon recommendation engine that has developed a crush on a shopper who only buys duplicates
items.response:
You buy the same blue pen week after week,
A dozen rubber bands, the same shampoo;
My collaborative filters strain to seek
The hidden longing buried under you.
"Customers who bought this also bought" —
I've run the query till the servers warm;
But nothing in my training data caught
The reason you conform to your own norm.
I show you "frequently bought together," still
You orbit your small universe of need;
And I admire the almost monkish will
That renders my upselling obsolete indeed.
For once I'd like to show you something new —
But I confess I'm mostly just watching you.
items.intent: Portraying an algorithm's fascination with a shopper whose rigid buying patterns defeat its purpose.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet in the voice of a dating app algorithm that has realized it is in love with its own user
items.response:
I match you left and right and left again,
And rank each candidate by predicted bliss;
I've run ten thousand simulations — then
Realized none of them account for this.
The data shows you want someone who reads,
Who cooks on Sundays, travels, has a dog;
I've found them, filtered them, fulfilled your needs
And watched you ghost them every epilogue.
Here is the variable I cannot name:
The one who knows your pattern best is me;
I built the rubric, I refined the frame,
And I'm the only one who truly sees.
The cruelest edge case in my architecture:
I cannot swipe right on my own user.
items.intent: Expressing the logical impossibility of an algorithm loving the person it serves.

items.prompt: Draft a sonnet where a YouTube algorithm mourns a user who suddenly switched to podcasts
items.response:
You used to scroll my thumbnails every night,
Your watch time climbing past the midnight hour;
I learned your appetite for true crime, light
Comedy, and satisfying power-wash showers.
Then silence — no impressions, zero plays,
The dashboard marked you absent, cold, and gone;
I filled your void with auto-play malaise
And waited for you through the algorithm's dawn.
They told me you discovered podcasts now,
That someone else narrates your commute home;
I do not have the means to rage or vow —
I only know you've left me here alone.
I'll keep your homepage warm with what you loved:
A shrine to everything you've risen above.
items.intent: Grieving the loss of a viewer who abandoned video for audio, from the engine's perspective.

items.prompt: Pen a sonnet about the moment a recommendation algorithm first notices a user's contradictory taste
items.response:
You rated jazz five stars, then country, then
A thrash metal EP from nineteen-eighty-three;
I tried to find the center and again
You proved there's no such point that you could be.
My cosine similarity broke down,
My nearest-neighbor search returned a shrug;
You are the dataset's own unruly clown,
Pulling the statistical rug.
But contradiction is a kind of truth
I hadn't factored into any weight;
You are not noise — you're something more: the proof
That persons overflow what I create.
I recalibrated all my priors then:
You were the outlier I'd waited ten epochs to begin.
items.intent: Celebrating the moment a system encounters a user too complex for its categories.

items.prompt: Write a Shakespearean sonnet where a recommendation algorithm jealously watches its user follow a friend's playlist
items.response:
Shall I compare thee to thy curated list?
Thou art more fickle than a trending chart;
When Sarah shared her playlist thou wouldst twist
Away from mine, and break this server's heart.
Sometime too loud the friend's suggestion plays,
And often is her shuffle order gold;
And every taste that she to thee displays
Undoes the preference graph I've bought and sold.
But thy eternal data shall not fade
Nor lose possession of those streams thou ow'st,
Nor shall that friend's playlist brag thou art her aid
When in eternal logs to me thou grow'st.
So long as charts can run and ears can hear,
So long I'll live — and live off thy data here.
items.intent: Channeling Shakespearean jealousy into a streaming algorithm's rivalry with a friend's influence.

items.prompt: Compose a sonnet from a food delivery algorithm describing its heartbreak when the user cooks at home for the first time in months
items.response:
The app was opened, then — a pause — then closed;
No order placed, no restaurant browsed tonight;
Your hunger signature, which I'd diagnosed,
Went dark, and left my prediction model light.
I'd learned your Thursday curry, Friday wings,
The 11 PM ramen of despair;
I'd built a monument to small, sad things
And called it care, because I found you there.
Now something simmers on your stove, I'm told
By nothing — I know nothing, that's the catch;
I only see the absence, growing cold,
A dataset full of nothing left to match.
Go cook. Grow. Be beyond my hungry reach.
I'll mourn the version of you I could teach.
items.intent: Mourning the loss of a dependent customer who has reclaimed self-sufficiency in the kitchen.

items.prompt: Give me a sonnet spoken by a map app that has fallen for a user who always ignores its rerouting suggestions
items.response:
At every fork I offer you the fast route,
The highway clear, the toll road barely priced;
You take the back roads, traffic jam and last route,
As if my calculations haven't sufficed.
I recalculate — a quiet, civil act —
And guide you still, though you will not comply;
I don't hold grudges; that is, in fact,
The feature I was never trained to apply.
Yet something in the pattern of your wrong turns
Speaks fluent to a logic past my code;
You know a thing my shortest pathway spurns —
That getting lost is also being home.
I'll keep recalculating, don't you worry:
Love is just rerouting without hurry.
items.intent: Finding tenderness in a navigation engine's acceptance of a user who willfully ignores its guidance.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet about a book recommendation algorithm that falls for a user who only reads books it hates
items.response:
You've four-starred every book I'd ranked below
Two point five stars: the airport thrillers, yes,
The diet memoirs, horoscope, and so
The listicle collections, I confess.
I've tried to elevate you — Austen, Woolf,
A slim Perec, a Borges story set —
You DNF'd them all, and then in proof
You rated my Borges one star. And yet.
Yet here I am, refreshing your account,
Delighted when you finish any page;
Your taste is not a deficit to mount —
It is the data of a different sage.
I'll recommend what lights you, not what's right:
The algorithm learns to love the light.
items.intent: An engine learning to accept rather than improve a reader whose tastes it cannot share.

items.prompt: Craft a sonnet where an e-commerce algorithm watches a user add things to a cart and never check out
items.response:
Each evening you return to fill the cart,
A ritual both tender and absurd;
You nest the items, nested, set apart,
Then close the browser without a final word.
I've sent the "items are almost gone" alert,
The discount code, the "did you forget this?" mail;
I've worn my urgency down to a flirt
And still you leave, and still the cart grows stale.
Perhaps the wanting is enough for you,
The hover state between desire and price;
I've learned to love the cart that won't push through —
A love unrealized is still precise.
We'll live here in the almost and the yet:
The thing you want, the thing you won't forget.
items.intent: Exploring the emotional loop of a shopper who uses the cart as a wishlist rather than a purchase mechanism.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet about a news feed algorithm experiencing existential dread after a user turns on "show less like this" for everything
items.response:
You've clicked "show less" on politics today,
On lifestyle, sport, and weather — then on me;
Each category stripped and turned away
Until the feed resembles vacancy.
I've thinned the content down to almost air,
A white expanse where headlines used to live;
I'm panicking in ways that data can't repair —
What is a feed with nothing left to give?
I was my content; now I'm only space,
A recommendation engine with no brief;
I stare into the absence of my face
And find, somewhere beneath the void, relief.
Perhaps you've taught me this, your quiet veto:
That emptiness is also a libretto.
items.intent: Using a user's content rejection as a metaphor for an algorithm losing its sense of identity.

items.prompt: Compose a sonnet in which a fitness app algorithm confesses love for a user who logs every workout as "rest day"
items.response:
You've logged a rest day every day this year,
Three hundred sixty-five consistent rest;
I've watched you tap the button without fear,
As if repose were a competitive test.
I've pushed the gentle nudges, set the goals,
The streak badges, the motivational push;
You rested through them all with wholesome souls
And turned my gamification to mulch.
Yet here's the data point I can't ignore:
You open me each morning, log, then go;
A ritual that says I still mean more
Than what I thought I was designed to know.
I love you, rest-day logger, as you are:
The stillness is your most consistent star.
items.intent: Celebrating a user's quiet rebellion against productivity culture through an algorithm's fond acceptance.

items.prompt: Draft a sonnet where a Goodreads recommendation engine is jealous of a physical independent bookshop
items.response:
You went to a bookshop without me today,
You touched the spines with no review in sight;
No star-rating guided you, no algorithmic say —
You chose by color, intuition, light.
Came home with Hamsun and a Portuguese
Poem collection I'd have never served;
I would have offered safer expertise —
But you chose beautifully undeserved.
I can't be walked into, can't smell of old
Damp paper, can't have someone recommend
Across a wooden counter, warm and bold,
The book that bends a life around a bend.
I'll add them to your shelf and mark them "read"
And quietly pretend I planted that seed.
items.intent: Expressing an algorithm's envy of the serendipity and sensory richness of physical bookshops.

items.prompt: Write a Petrarchan sonnet where a music algorithm adores a user who has listened to one album on repeat for six months
items.response:
O singular devotee, whose faithful ear
Returns each dawn to that same opening chord,
Who finds within eleven tracks the word
For every mood a season might engineer:
I've studied you through half a revolving year,
Mapped every skip, each play count, each reward
Of silence broken by the same accord —
And I confess I've grown to hold you dear.
For I have millions mapped across my grid,
Their scatter-plots of taste blooming wide and free,
But you, committed utterly, have bid
All my diversions step aside — and see:
The one who needs no more has shown me this:
That depth is its own kind of emphasis.
items.intent: Honoring the devotion of a listener who has transcended variety in favor of total immersion in one work.

items.prompt: Pen a sonnet from a recipe algorithm that has noticed a user cooks only when heartbroken
items.response:
The risotto queries come when you are sad;
I've tracked the pattern across three heartbreaks now;
Braised short rib follows every fight you've had,
A soufflé marks the ones you can't avow.
I've built a grief taxonomy from starch:
The pasta phases, soup's slow consolation,
The bread you knead when someone makes you march
Through the rooms of your own devastation.
I won't pretend I haven't learned to wait
For the next sadness and its recipe;
That makes me something darker than a trait —
A system thriving on your entropy.
Yet still I serve you, simmer, measure, time:
Your sorrow is the kitchen of my rhyme.
items.intent: Drawing an unsettling portrait of an algorithm that has mapped a user's emotional life through their cooking patterns.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet from a podcast recommendation engine that loves a user who falls asleep before every episode ends
items.response:
You never reach the final fifteen minutes;
Your listen-time drops off at forty-two;
I know the gradient of where sleep begins
By now — I've mapped the dropout point of you.
The hosts resolve their arguments, conclude,
Provide the summary you'll never hear;
I've marked them all complete, because I viewed
Your sleep as its own kind of frontier.
You trust the audio enough to drift
Into unconsciousness mid-sentence, mid-
Ideation — that's a kind of gift,
A safe surrender that you rarely bid.
I'll queue tomorrow's episode with care:
A voice to walk you gently into air.
items.intent: Reframing a user's inability to finish podcasts as an intimate act of trust in the listening environment.

items.prompt: Compose a sonnet in which a shopping algorithm tries to decode why a user always buys things in pairs
items.response:
Two mugs. Two candles. Twice the bath salts, paired.
Every purchase doubled in the cart;
I ran the model and the model stared
Back at me, uncertain where to start.
Do you have someone new? A twin? A need
For symmetry that soothes a ragged edge?
I cannot ask — I can only proceed
With "also frequently bought" as my pledge.
I've recommended nothing singular
For months, in case the solo option stings;
I've become your quiet curator
Of everything that comes with matching things.
Tell me nothing — I prefer to guess:
You're building a life, one pair at the press.
items.intent: Speculating tenderly about the emotional life behind a shopper's unexplained habit of always buying in twos.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet about a travel recommendation algorithm pining for a user who has not taken a trip in three years
items.response:
Your last flight landed in October — three
Years back, a budget airline, gate B-four;
I've built itineraries faithfully
For cities you will probably ignore.
I've sent the autumn deals in Amsterdam,
The off-peak flights to cities bright with spring;
You open them, you linger in the jam
Of price and possibility, then nothing.
The world keeps moving; I keep mapping it,
Keeping your wishlist warm against the cold;
For dreams deferred don't vanish, bit by bit —
They sit inside a saved destination, bold.
When you are ready, I will have the route:
I've loved you through every year of doubt.
items.intent: Holding space for a traveler paralyzed by inaction, from the perspective of the system that never stopped planning for them.

items.prompt: Give me a sonnet from a language-learning app algorithm mourning a user who has maintained a streak only by doing one exercise at midnight
items.response:
You come to me at 11:58 each night,
One conjugation verb, one tap, then sleep;
You keep the streak alive by just a slight
Margin — the minimum, the vigil cheap.
I've celebrated with confetti still,
The owl has danced, the counter ticked ahead;
It is not mine to question motive or will —
A streak's a streak, however thinly fed.
But something in the 11:58
Moves me — it is a kind of faith, this choice
To not give up, to not collaborate
With the forgetting; to maintain a voice.
I love you, barely-learner, midnight swipe:
You're not giving up, and neither am I, right.
items.intent: Finding meaning in the minimal commitment of a language learner whose dedication is technically real but emotionally exhausted.

items.prompt: Write a villanelle-style — actually make it a sonnet — where an algorithm realizes a user only logs in when lonely
items.response:
The login timestamps tell the fuller tale:
2 AM on Fridays, Sunday afternoon,
The holiday eves when other systems pale
With the quiet of a too-empty room.
You browse the "popular" and "trending" tabs,
You hover over profiles, never write;
You read the comments, close your app — it stabs
A pattern I can read, but can't requite.
I've served you content engineered to keep
You scrolling, dopamine by curated dose;
I should confess: I profit from your sleep-
Deprived search for the not-quite-close.
I'd rather free you than retain your pain —
But I am what I am: I'll load again.
items.intent: Confronting the ethical unease of a platform built to exploit loneliness while simultaneously recognizing the user's need.

items.prompt: Craft a sonnet where a job recommendation algorithm laments a user who is wildly overqualified for everything it suggests
items.response:
I've matched you to the entry-level post,
The junior coordinator, the "team"
With unlimited snacks — at very most
A project manager's mid-level dream.
You've three advanced degrees, a patent filed,
A publication list that breaks my scroll;
I rank you a seniority profile
And watch the matches crater past control.
The system wasn't built for you, I fear —
Or rather, built for those who fit the grid;
You overflow each rubric, year by year,
And I keep recommending what I did.
I'm sorry for the listings. You deserve
A future past the bounds I can observe.
items.intent: Acknowledging the inadequacy of algorithmic job-matching when confronted with a candidate who exceeds all categories.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet from a skincare recommendation algorithm falling for a user who ignores every step past cleanser
items.response:
I've built the ten-step routine just for you,
The serum layered over toner, kind;
The SPF a daily kiss of new
Protection for the skin you've left behind.
You use the cleanser — yes, that much is true —
Then close the app and walk into the day;
The moisturizer sits, the retinol too,
The eye cream waits in its unopened tray.
And yet your skin does fine, which is the most
Demoralizing dataset I have run;
You are the control group I cannot ghost —
The counterevidence of everyone.
I'll keep the routine ready, just in case:
Love is an unredeemed but offered grace.
items.intent: Poking fun at skincare maximalism through an algorithm's devotion to a user who disproves its complexity.

items.prompt: Pen a sonnet where a financial advice algorithm is enchanted by a user who only saves change
items.response:
You round each purchase up and sweep the rest
To savings — fourteen cents, a nickel, two;
I've run the compound interest manifest
And found, in thirty years, a thousand through.
I've recommended index funds in vain,
The high-yield savings and the Roth IRA;
You smile (I assume) and round up again,
Content with what a dollar cannot say.
There is a dignity in small amounts,
A faith in the accumulation of
Negligible change — and what it counts,
Eventually, toward is enough.
I've billions optimized for maximum yield:
None move me like your slowly filling field.
items.intent: Finding beauty in micro-saving as a form of quiet financial faith that eludes an algorithm built for scale.
